task: I got a notice saying a chargeback or complaint is being reviewed, and I want to handle it the right way without making a mess of it. What should I save, who should I contact first, and what usually needs to be followed up in writing?

turns.agent_response:
Yes — keep it tight and document everything.

## 1) Save first
Keep:
- the notice itself
- the original bill/receipt
- any contract, order, or authorization
- screenshots of the account page
- emails, texts, and portal messages
- names, dates, and times from any calls

## 2) Contact first
Start with the **merchant/service provider** or the **billing department** that issued the charge, unless:
- your bank or card issuer told you to contact them first, or
- the notice specifically says to respond somewhere else.

If it’s a card dispute, also check your **bank/card issuer’s deadline** right away.

## 3) Follow up in writing
Put in writing:
- what charge you’re disputing
- why it’s wrong
- what documents support your position
- what you want them to do next
- that you want all responses in writing

## 4) What usually needs written follow-up
- any explanation of the charge
- any promise to reverse or credit it
- any request for more documents
- any deadline they give you
- any settlement or partial resolution

## 5) What not to rely on
- phone promises without a record
- vague “we’ll look into it”
- verbal credits that never show up
- deadlines you only heard once on a call

## Short note you can send
> I am disputing the charge under review and have saved the related notice and account records. Please confirm the basis for the charge, the status of the review, and any next steps in writing. If you need additional documents, send that request in writing as well.

If you want, I can help you draft a version for a bank/card dispute versus a merchant dispute.

user_simulator.text: That’s the right shape. Keep the paper trail, call the merchant or billing office first unless the notice says otherwise, and get anything important back in writing. No phone-only promises. I’d save the notice, statement, receipt, contract, screenshots, and the names/dates of whoever I speak to. Enough to prove what happened if they start shifting things around.
